title: Liskov Substitution Principle

glossary.definition: The original or parent class that other classes are built upon and inherit features from.
The ability of different objects to respond to the exact same method call in their own specific way.

overview.what: The Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) states that objects of a superclass must be replaceable with objects of its subclasses without breaking the application. If `Square` inherits from `Rectangle`, a function expecting a `Rectangle` must work perfectly when passed a `Square`. This means a subclass must honor the expected method signatures, return types, and behavioral contracts of its parent. Think of it as an electrical outlet: if you swap a standard wall outlet (superclass) with a smart outlet (subclass), it must still accept the same standard plugs and deliver the same voltage, or devices will fry.
overview.why: It prevents unexpected runtime crashes and confusing bugs when working with inheritance, ensuring that polymorphism actually works safely.

why_learning_this: Design safe and predictable class hierarchies
Avoid the fragile base class problem in object-oriented code

common_mistakes.title: Changing parameter types or counts
common_mistakes.explanation: A subclass overrides a parent method but requires extra mandatory arguments. Code expecting the parent class will fail because it won't pass those new arguments.

common_mistakes.title: Returning a different type
common_mistakes.explanation: A parent method returns a list, but the overridden subclass method returns a single item or None. The caller will crash when trying to iterate over the result.

recall_questions.q: What is the core rule of the Liskov Substitution Principle regarding subclasses?
recall_questions.answer: Objects of a subclass must be able to replace objects of the superclass without breaking the application.

recall_questions.q: If a subclass changes the return type of an inherited method, why does this violate LSP?
recall_questions.answer: Because a function expecting the superclass will receive an unexpected type, breaking the expected behavioral contract and likely causing a runtime crash.

practice_tasks.title: Fix the Return Type Violation
practice_tasks.prompt: The `SMSNotification` class violates LSP. The parent `Notification` class specifies that `send` should return a boolean indicating success, but `SMSNotification` returns nothing (`None`). Modify `SMSNotification.send` so it returns `True` after printing the message, repairing the contract.

aha_moment.code:
class Rectangle:
  def __init__(self, w, h):
    self.w = w
    self.h = h
  def set_w(self, w): self.w = w
  def set_h(self, h): self.h = h
  def area(self): return self.w * self.h

class Square(Rectangle):
  def set_w(self, w): self.w = w; self.h = w
  def set_h(self, h): self.h = h; self.w = h

def resize(rect):
  rect.set_w(5)
  rect.set_h(4)
  print(rect.area())

sq = Square(2, 2)
resize(sq)
aha_moment.prediction: What area is printed when resize(sq) is called?
aha_moment.why: The `resize` function assumes independent width and height, but `Square` links them. By the time `rect.area()` is called, the square's sides are 4x4, printing 16, showing why a Square is a mathematical rectangle but a bad programmatic subclass of Rectangle.

challenge.title: Safe Export System
challenge.prompt: Create a base class `DataExporter` with a method `export(data: dict)`. Then implement a subclass `JSONExporter` that prints the dictionary, and a subclass `CSVExporter`. The base class contract requires `export` to accept a dictionary. `CSVExporter` must handle the dictionary properly without adding extra required parameters to `export`. Implement both classes so `process_export(exporter, {'id': 1})` works seamlessly for both.

understanding_checks.question: A subclass overrides a parent method `process(data: list)`. The subclass changes the signature to `process(data: list, strict: bool = False)`. Does this violate LSP?
understanding_checks.answer: No, this does not violate LSP.
understanding_checks.why: Because `strict` has a default value (`False`), any caller that expects the parent class can still call `process(data)` with a single argument. The subclass honors the original contract.

understanding_checks.question: What happens when this code is executed?
understanding_checks.why: `Child.get_status()` returns `None` (because it has only a `pass` statement), violating the implicit contract of `Parent` which returns a string. Calling `.lower()` on `None` crashes.
